New message
PB phoenix@untitledui.com ×LS lana@untitledui.com ×

Anatomy & behaviour

How a modal is composed, and the rules it follows.

Architecture

Component architecture

A complete modal is composed of four nested sub-components, plus an optional close button.

DialogTrigger          // manages open / close state
  ModalOverlay         // backdrop + positioning layer
    Modal              // container with enter / exit animation
      Dialog           // role="dialog", focus trap
        CloseButton    // optional manual close
        Header / Body / Footer
Anatomy

Anatomy elements

Each modal is built from these parts — some required, some optional.

ElementDescriptionRequired
Backdrop / overlaySemi-transparent dimmed layer behind the modalYes
Modal containerThe surface card that holds all contentYes
Close button (×)Top-right icon button to dismissRecommended
Featured icon / imageVisual element at the top (icon, avatar, photo)Optional
TitlePrimary label describing the purposeYes
Supporting textSecondary description below the titleOptional
Body / contentForms, lists, media, settingsConditional
Footer / actionsPrimary + secondary CTA buttonsRecommended
States

Modal-level states

Every modal moves through a predictable lifecycle, plus async and locked states.

StateDescriptionVisual indicator
ClosedNot renderedNot visible
OpeningEnter animation playingFade + zoom-in
OpenFully visible, interactiveBackdrop + modal rendered
ClosingExit animation playingFade + zoom-out
LoadingContent fetchingSkeleton / spinner in body
ErrorContent failed to loadError message + retry
Non-dismissableCannot be closed by the userNo close button, Esc disabled
Sizes

Sizes & layout

Seven width tokens cover everything from a confirmation prompt to a full-screen overlay. Height is auto by default, capped at ~90vh with internal scroll.

xs
~320px
sm
~480px
md
~560px
lg
~640px
xl
~768px
2xl
~900px
full
100vw
Motion

Animation & transitions

The overlay fades while the container zooms. Enter is ~200ms ease-out; exit is ~150ms ease-in.

/* overlay */
enter: opacity 0 -> 1   (200ms ease-out)
exit:  opacity 1 -> 0   (150ms ease-in)

/* container */
enter: scale(.95) opacity 0 -> scale(1) opacity 1   (200ms)
exit:  scale(1) opacity 1 -> scale(.95) opacity 0   (150ms)
Accessibility

Accessibility

WCAG 2.1 AA compliant via React Aria — focus trapping, keyboard navigation and the correct ARIA roles are built in.

  • Role dialog with aria-modal="true", labelled by the title and described by the supporting text.
  • Focus moves to the first focusable element on open and is trapped while the modal is open.
  • Focus returns to the trigger element when the modal closes.
  • Esc closes dismissable modals; Tab / Shift+Tab cycle interactive elements.
  • Body scroll is locked while the modal is open; error messages link to inputs via aria-describedby.
API

Props & API reference

The key props across the four sub-components.

PropComponentTypeDefault
isOpenDialogTriggerboolean—
defaultOpenDialogTriggerbooleanfalse
onOpenChangeDialogTrigger(open: boolean) => void—
isDismissableModalOverlaybooleantrue
isKeyboardDismissDisabledModalOverlaybooleanfalse
aria-labelDialogstring—
onPressCloseButton() => void—
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens the modal reads — measured from the live component and mapped onto Tungo’s palette.

Panel background--overlay (white in light)
Title text--txt
Supporting text--txt-2
Border / divider--hair
Brand / primary--magenta
Destructive#F0433A
Border radius16px (panel) / 8px (buttons, fields)
Elevation0 20px 28px -6px rgba(0,0,0,.42)
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use modals for focused tasks needing input or confirmation.
  • Include a visible close button in most modals.
  • Keep content concise — avoid scrolling where possible.
  • Match the intent (default / warning / destructive) to the action.
  • Trap focus, lock body scroll, and return focus on close.
Don’t
  • Nest multiple modals — use a single modal with steps.
  • Use a modal for non-critical info that can live inline.
  • Trigger modals automatically without user action.
  • Remove the close button from dismissable modals.
  • Overload a modal — reach for a drawer or full page instead.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use the isOpen and onOpenChange props on DialogTrigger to open and close programmatically and respond to every dismissal event.

Set isDismissable={false} on ModalOverlay. This hides the close affordance, blocks backdrop-click close, and disables the Escape key.

DialogTrigger wraps ModalOverlay, Modal and Dialog; add CloseButton for a manual close control.

Override the enter / exit transition classes on ModalOverlay (backdrop) and Modal (container) with utilities or custom CSS.

Yes — built on React Aria with focus trapping, keyboard navigation, correct ARIA attributes and screen-reader announcements.

Yes. Modals are responsive; on small viewports consider switching to a bottom-sheet pattern below the sm breakpoint.

Notifications

Powerful and customizable toast notification components for modern apps and websites — transient, non-blocking messages that slide in to confirm an action, report a result, or surface an update. Featured-icon, gray, brand, error, warning, success, progress, image and avatar variants. Built with Sonner, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Notification example

The flagship toast — a featured icon, title, supporting text and a muted + brand action pair, with a close button.

Version 1.4.1 is now available
Includes the all new dashboard view. Pages and exports will now load faster.
Click to trigger a toast
Get started

Installation

You can add this notifications component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add notifications
// npm install sonner, then mount the Toaster in your root layout
import { Toaster, toast } from "sonner";
<Toaster position="bottom-right" />

Notifications examples

Below are examples and variations of this notifications component. Use the close button or a muted action to dismiss a toast, then trigger it again to replay the animation.

Variant
Get the code

Icon default

A neutral featured icon in a rounded square — used for general product and app updates.

Version 1.4.1 is now available
Includes the all new dashboard view. Pages and exports will now load faster.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Icon gray

A neutral info circle — for informational updates with no specific sentiment.

We’ve just released a new feature
Check out the all new dashboard view. Pages and exports now load faster.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Icon brand

A brand-coloured info circle — for branded announcements and feature highlights.

We’ve just released a new feature
Check out the all new dashboard view. Pages and exports now load faster.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Icon error

A red alert circle with a single action — for failures and destructive events.

This project has been unpublished
Removing all users has unpublished this project. Add users to republish.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Icon warning

An amber alert circle with a single action — for cautionary, non-blocking states.

This project has been unpublished
Removing all users has unpublished this project. Add users to republish.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Icon success

A green check circle — for successful, user-initiated operations.

Successfully updated profile
Your changes have been saved and your profile is live. Your team can make edits.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Progress notification

A progress bar with a percentage label — for async tasks like uploads and exports.

Uploading ‘website-FINAL06.fig’
Please wait while we upload your file.
75% uploaded…
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Image notification

A thumbnail replaces the icon area — for rich, marketing-style announcements.

We’ve just released a new update!
Check out the all new dashboard view. Pages and exports now load faster.
Click to trigger a toast
Variant
Get the code

Avatar notification

A user avatar with a presence dot and timestamp — for social and collaborative events.

Katherine Moss2 mins ago
I’ve finished adding my notes. Happy for us to review whenever you’re ready!
Click to trigger a toast

Anatomy & behaviour

How a toast is composed, and the rules it follows.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

Each toast is built from these parts — some required, some optional.

PartDescriptionRequired
ContainerSurface card with radius, border and elevationYes
Icon / avatarLeft-side visual: featured icon, image thumbnail or avatarOptional
TitleBold, short headlineYes
DescriptionSecondary body text (1–2 lines)Optional
Action buttonsOne or two text-style (ghost / link) buttonsOptional
Close button (×)Top-right dismiss controlYes
Progress barHorizontal bar below the description (progress variant)Optional
States

States & auto-dismiss

Toasts are transient. Informational toasts auto-dismiss; error, warning and progress toasts wait for the user.

StateBehaviourAuto-dismiss
Default / gray / brandInformational update~4–5s
SuccessConfirms a completed action~4–5s
Error / warningRequires a manual decisionNever — manual only
ProgressStays until complete or cancelledOn completion
HoverPauses the auto-dismiss timerPaused
ExitSlides out and fades, then unmounts—
Motion

Motion & stacking

Toasts slide in from the edge and fade out on dismiss. Multiple toasts stack with the newest on top.

/* enter */
translateY(100%) opacity 0 -> translateY(0) opacity 1   (300ms ease-out)

/* exit */
translateY(0) opacity 1 -> translateY(-16px) opacity 0   (200ms ease-in)

/* respects prefers-reduced-motion: fade only, no translate */
Accessibility

Accessibility

Toasts announce themselves to assistive tech and never rely on colour alone.

  • role="status" with aria-live="polite" for info; role="alert" / assertive for errors.
  • The close button is labelled “Dismiss notification” and has a 44×44px touch target.
  • Esc closes the active toast; all interactive elements show a visible focus ring.
  • Icons always reinforce meaning beyond colour, and body text stays at 12px or larger.
  • Informational toasts stay at least 4 seconds (WCAG 2.2.1); motion respects prefers-reduced-motion.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a toast reads, mapped onto Tungo’s palette.

Container background--overlay (white in light)
Title text--txt
Body text--txt-2
Border--hair-2
Brand action / progress--magenta
Error icon#F0433A
Warning icon#F0A12E
Success / presence dot#19B36B
Border radius12px (container) / 8px (icon)
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use error for failures, success for completed actions, warning for cautions.
  • Use progress for async tasks and avatar for user-to-user events.
  • Keep titles under ~60 characters and bodies under two lines.
  • Limit to one or two action buttons, positioned consistently (bottom-right).
  • Auto-dismiss informational toasts after 4–5 seconds.
Don’t
  • Use toasts for critical blocking errors — reach for a modal.
  • Stack more than three or four toasts at once.
  • Put form inputs inside a notification.
  • Auto-dismiss error or warning toasts.
  • Rely on colour alone, or use text below 12px.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Call toast() from Sonner — or toast.success(), toast.error(), toast.warning() — passing a title, description and optional action.

Wherever the Toaster is positioned. Bottom-right is recommended on desktop; the toast slides up from the bottom on mobile.

Informational toasts auto-dismiss after about 4–5 seconds. Error, warning and progress toasts stay until the user acts.

Yes — the progress variant renders a track and fill below the description, driven by a value you update or by toast.promise().

Yes — they use the correct ARIA roles and live regions, are keyboard-dismissable, and respect reduced-motion preferences.

Sonner stacks them automatically with the newest on top; keep it to three or four visible at once.

Page headers

Powerful and customizable page header components for modern apps and websites — the top section of a page that anchors a breadcrumb, a title or user identity, primary actions and search. Simple, avatar, banner and centered layouts. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Page header example

The flagship pattern — a gradient banner with an overlapping avatar, breadcrumb, identity, a secondary + primary action group and a search field.

Settings…Team
Olivia Rhye
olivia@untitledui.com
⌘K
Get started

Installation

These page header components don’t require any installation — just copy and paste into your project, or scaffold one with the CLI.

npx tungo-ui@latest add page-header
// copy the PageHeader compound component into your project
import { PageHeader } from "@/components/application/page-header";

Page header examples

Below are examples and variations of this page header component.

Variant
Get the code

Simple

Breadcrumb, page title and supporting text on the left; actions and search on the right.

Settings…Team
Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Avatar

An inline avatar with the user’s name and email in place of a page title.

Settings…Team
Olivia Rhye
olivia@untitledui.com
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Banner simple

A gradient banner sits above a simple title header.

Settings…Team
Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Banner avatar

A gradient banner with an avatar overlapping its lower edge, beside the identity block.

Settings…Team
Olivia Rhye
olivia@untitledui.com
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Banner simple centered

A banner header with the breadcrumb, identity and actions centred.

Settings…Team
Olivia Rhye
olivia@untitledui.com
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Banner avatar centered

A banner header with a centred, overlapping avatar and centred actions.

Settings…Team
Olivia Rhye
olivia@untitledui.com
⌘K

Anatomy & building blocks

How a page header is composed, and the rules it follows.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

A page header mixes and matches these parts — only the title or identity is strictly required.

PartDescriptionRequired
BannerOptional gradient or image strip across the topOptional
BreadcrumbTrail showing the page’s location in the hierarchyRecommended
AvatarUser photo with an optional verified badgeOptional
Title / namePage title or the user’s display nameYes
Supporting textA short description or the user’s emailOptional
ActionsOne or more secondary buttons plus a primary buttonOptional
SearchA keyboard-shortcut search field (⌘K)Optional
DividerHairline separating the header from page contentRecommended
Layout

Layout options

The same building blocks support six common arrangements.

VariantBannerAvatarAlignment
Simple––Left
Avatar–InlineLeft
Banner simpleYes–Left
Banner avatarYesOverlappingLeft
Banner simple centeredYes–Centered
Banner avatar centeredYesOverlappingCentered
Accessibility

Accessibility

The header is the landmark for the page, so its structure matters for assistive tech.

  • The breadcrumb is wrapped in nav with aria-label="Breadcrumb"; the current page is marked aria-current="page".
  • The page title is a single h1 so screen-reader users land on the right heading.
  • The search field has an associated label and announces its ⌘K shortcut.
  • All buttons, links and the search field show a visible focus ring and are reachable by keyboard.
  • The banner is decorative — it carries no essential text and is hidden from assistive tech.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a page header reads.

Title / name--txt
Supporting text--txt-2
Breadcrumb--txt-3 / --txt-2 on hover
Primary action--magenta
Search / field surface--card
Avatar ring & divider--card / --hair
Verified badge#1d9bf0
BannerDecorative gradient (theme-independent)
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Keep one clear primary action; demote the rest to secondary.
  • Use the avatar variants for profile and account pages.
  • Keep titles short and let supporting text carry the detail.
  • Use the breadcrumb to show where the page sits.
  • Reserve centered layouts for lighter, profile-style pages.
Don’t
  • Crowd the header with more than one primary button.
  • Use more than one h1 — the title is the page heading.
  • Put essential information inside the decorative banner.
  • Stack many actions on narrow screens — collapse into a menu.
  • Mix centered and left-aligned headers across the same product.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use a banner for profile, team and account pages where a little visual identity helps; keep the simple header for dense, task-focused pages.

Use the title variant for section pages (“Team members”) and the avatar variant when the page represents a specific person.

Aim for one primary action plus one or two secondary actions; move overflow into a menu on smaller screens.

Yes — an optional search field with a ⌘K shortcut is included; omit it where page-level search isn’t needed.

Yes. Below the small breakpoint the actions and search stack to full width and the layout collapses to a single column.

Paginations

Free and open-source pagination components for modern apps and websites — navigate multi-page content with numbered pages, previous / next controls, jump-to-first/last, page-size selectors, go-to-page inputs, and dot or line indicators. Page, card, button-group, dot and line families. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Pagination example

The flagship pattern — a Previous / Next pair on the edges with centred numbered pages and an ellipsis for large ranges.

…
Get started

Installation

You can add this pagination component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add pagination
// copy the pagination components into your project
import * as Paginations from "@/components/application/pagination";

Pagination examples

Below are examples and variations of this pagination component. Click a page, the arrows, or a dot / line to move the active state.

Page
Get the code

Page default

Previous / Next on the edges, centred numbered pages.

…
Page
Get the code

Page minimal center

Bordered Previous / Next buttons with centred numbered pages.

…
Card
Get the code

Card default

The page pattern inside a card / table footer with a top divider.

…
Card
Get the code

Card minimal right aligned

Page info and a page-size selector on the left; Previous / Next on the right.

Page 1 of 10
Card
Get the code

Card minimal center aligned

Previous / Next on the edges with page info and page-size centred.

Page 1 of 10
Card
Get the code

Card minimal left aligned

Previous / Next on the left; page-size and page info on the right.

Page 1 of 10
Button group
Get the code

Button group right aligned

A connected button group, right aligned.

Button group
Get the code

Button group center aligned

A connected button group, centred.

Button group
Get the code

Button group left aligned

A connected button group, left aligned.

Advanced
Get the code

Card advanced

A go-to-page input and rows-per-page selector with jump-to-first/last and numbered pages.

Pageof 10Rows per page
…
Advanced
Get the code

Card advanced center

The advanced pattern with the numbered pages centred and rows-per-page on the right.

Pageof 10
…
Rows per page
Indicator
Get the code

Pagination dot

A dot-style position indicator for carousels and sliders, in two sizes.

Indicator
Get the code

Pagination line

A line / tab-style position indicator for carousels and sliders, in two sizes.

Anatomy & families

How pagination is composed, and which family to reach for.

Families

Five families

FamilyUse case
PageClassic numbered pagination with Previous / Next text buttons
CardPagination inside a card or table footer with a page-size selector
Button groupPagination rendered as a single connected control
DotDot-style position indicator for carousels and sliders
LineLine / tab-style position indicator for carousels and sliders
Anatomy

Anatomy elements

ElementDescriptionWhere
Previous / NextText or bordered buttons that step one pageMost variants
Numbered pagesOne button per page; the active page is highlightedPage / card / group
EllipsisCollapses a long range; not interactiveLarge ranges
Jump first / last (« »)Icon buttons to the first or last pageCard advanced
Page info“Page 1 of 10” status textCard / advanced
Page-size selectorRows-per-page dropdown (10 / 25 / 50 / 100)Card / advanced
Go-to-page inputType a page number to jump directlyCard advanced
Dot / line segmentOne focusable indicator per slideDot / line
States

States

Every interactive element exposes the same set of states.

StateTreatment
DefaultMuted text on a transparent surface
HoverSubtle raised background fill
Active / current pageBrand-tinted background and brand text
DisabledGreyed out — Previous on page 1, Next on the last page
FocusVisible focus ring, never suppressed
Sizes

Sizes

Page buttons are a fixed 40×40px target; indicators come in two sizes.

Page button40×40px, 6–8px radius
Prev / Next padding14px / 8px
Dot — md / lg8px / 10px
Line — md / lg4px / 6px tall
Card padding16–24px / 12–16px
Keyboard

Keyboard navigation

KeyAction
TabMove focus to the next interactive element
Enter / SpaceActivate the focused button
← / →Move between page buttons in a button group
Home / EndJump to the first or last button in a group
EscClose the page-size dropdown
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The pagination is wrapped in nav with aria-label="Pagination".
  • The current page is marked aria-current="page" so it is announced as selected.
  • Previous / Next are disabled at the bounds rather than hidden, so focus order stays stable.
  • Dot and line segments are individual buttons with labels like “Go to page 2”.
  • Focus rings are always visible for keyboard users and never suppressed.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens pagination reads.

Page number text--txt-2
Active page--magenta tint + text
Hover background--raised
Prev / Next--txt-2
Disabled--txt-3
Card border / divider--hair-2
Input / dropdown surface--card
Indicators--magenta active / --hair idle
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Always show the current page with a clear active state.
  • Disable Previous on page 1 and Next on the last page.
  • Show the total page count, and an ellipsis for large ranges.
  • Use consistent page-size options (10, 25, 50, 100).
  • Use dots and lines only for carousels and sliders.
Don’t
  • Hide the current-page indicator.
  • Leave navigation buttons enabled with nowhere to go.
  • Render 50 individual page buttons instead of an ellipsis.
  • Use arbitrary page sizes like 7, 13 or 42.
  • Use dots or lines for primary content navigation.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use Page for article lists and search results, Card inside tables and data grids, and Button group when pagination sits in a toolbar.

In data-heavy views — pair the card and advanced variants with a rows-per-page dropdown whenever lists exceed ~50 rows.

Carousel and slider position indicators only — keep them to about seven or eight segments and never use them for content navigation.

An ellipsis collapses the middle of the range so you show the first pages, the last page, and the current neighbourhood.

Yes — it uses a labelled nav landmark, marks the current page with aria-current, keeps disabled bounds in the focus order, and preserves visible focus rings.

Progress steps

Powerful and customizable progress step components for modern apps and websites — they guide people through a multi-step flow, showing what’s completed, what’s active and what’s still ahead. Horizontal and vertical orientations; icon, number, featured-icon, minimal and text-with-line styles. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Progress steps example

The flagship pattern — numbered horizontal steps with a completed check, an active step and upcoming steps, each with a label and sublabel.

Your details
Name and email
2
Company details
Website and location
3
Invite your team
Start collaborating
4
Add your socials
Automatic sharing
Get started

Installation

You can add this progress steps component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add progress-steps
// copy the ProgressSteps component into your project
import { ProgressSteps } from "@/components/application/progress-steps";

Progress steps examples

Below are examples and variations of this progress steps component. Click any step to move the active state — earlier steps complete, later ones reset.

Horizontal
Get the code

Icon centered

Circular indicators on a connecting line with centred labels.

Your details
Name and email
Company details
Website and location
Invite your team
Start collaborating
Add your socials
Automatic sharing
Horizontal
Get the code

Icon centered with number

The same layout with a completed check and numbered upcoming steps.

Your details
Name and email
2
Company details

Add your socials
Automatic sharing

Get the code

Featured icon centered

Featured icons in rounded squares connected by a line.

Your details

Icon with text

A vertical flow with dot indicators, titles and descriptions.

Your details
Please provide your name and email
Company details

A vertical flow with a completed check and numbered steps.

Featured icon with text

A vertical flow with featured icons, titles and descriptions.

Your details

Minimal
Get the code

Minimal icons

A compact dot indicator with a “Step X of Y” counter.

Step 2 of 4
Minimal
Get the code

Minimal icons connected

Compact dots joined by a connecting line.

Horizontal
Get the code

Text with line

A top-bar style where each step is marked by a coloured line above its label.

Your details
Name and email
Company details
Website and location
Invite your team
Start collaborating
Add your socials
Automatic sharing
Minimal
Get the code

Minimal icons connected

The connected dot indicator in a larger size.

Anatomy & states

How a progress step is composed, and what each state communicates.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

PartDescription
Step indicatorCircular badge holding an icon, check, number or dot
ConnectorLine or bar between indicators (horizontal or vertical)
LabelPrimary step name, semibold
SublabelSecondary description
Inner dotSmall filled dot inside the active / upcoming ring
Step counter“Step X of Y” text, minimal variant only
States

Step states

StateIndicatorLabel
CompletedGreen fill with a white check; connector colouredMuted, semibold
ActiveBrand fill with a white inner dot and focus ringBrand, semibold
UpcomingSurface with a 1.5px ring and a muted inner dotMuted
Far upcomingSame as upcoming at 60% opacityVery muted
DisabledSurface ring at 60% opacity, not navigableVery muted
Orientation

Orientation & variants

Nine variants across two orientations cover everything from a full wizard to a compact mobile indicator.

VariantOrientationIndicator
Icon centeredHorizontalDot
Icon centered with numberHorizontalNumber
Featured icon centeredHorizontalFeatured icon
Icon with textVerticalDot
Icon with numberVerticalNumber
Featured icon with textVerticalFeatured icon
Minimal iconsCompactDot + counter
Minimal icons connectedCompactDot + line
Text with lineHorizontalTop bar
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The step list uses role="tablist" with each step as a tab, labelled for screen readers.
  • The active step carries aria-current="step"; unreachable steps use aria-disabled.
  • Arrow keys move between steps (←/→ horizontal, ↑/↓ vertical); Enter or Space selects.
  • Completed and current states are announced — “Step 2 of 4, current step”.
  • State is never conveyed by colour alone: checks, dots and numbers reinforce meaning.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens progress steps read.

Completed fill#19B36B + white check
Active fill--magenta + white dot
Upcoming ring--card / --hair
Completed connector--magenta
Upcoming connector--hair
Active label--magenta
Label / sublabel--txt-2 / --txt-3
Featured icon box--card surface
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Always show which step is active and which are complete.
  • Let users navigate back to completed steps.
  • Use vertical or minimal variants on small screens.
  • Keep labels short and put detail in the sublabel.
  • Use featured icons when each step has a clear identity.
Don’t
  • Hide the current-step indicator.
  • Let users skip ahead to steps they can’t complete yet.
  • Cram a long horizontal flow onto a narrow screen.
  • Rely on colour alone to show completion.
  • Use more steps than the flow genuinely needs.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use horizontal for short wizards with room to spread out; switch to vertical or minimal on narrow and mobile layouts.

Yes — completed steps are navigable, so people can return to review or edit earlier input.

When each step has a distinct identity (details, company, team, socials) that an icon communicates faster than text.

Space-constrained or mobile contexts — a compact dot indicator, optionally with a “Step X of Y” counter or a connecting line.

Yes — it follows the WAI-ARIA steps pattern with a labelled tablist, aria-current on the active step, and full keyboard navigation.

Section footers

Powerful and customizable section footer components for modern apps and websites — horizontal action bars rendered at the bottom of a card or page section, separated from the content by a top border. They pair contextual filters and links on the left with secondary and primary actions on the right. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Section footer example

The flagship pattern — a segmented button group and a “Learn more” link on the left, with a secondary and primary action on the right.

Learn more
Get started

Installation

You can add this section footer component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add section-footer
// copy the SectionFooter component into your project
import { SectionFooter } from "@/components/application/section-footer";

Section footer examples

Below are examples and variations of this section footer component. The button group is a single-select control — click an option to switch it.

Variant
Get the code

Section footer button group

A segmented button group and a link on the left; secondary and primary actions on the right.

Learn more
Variant
Get the code

Section footer

The minimal variant — a single link on the left, actions on the right.

Learn more
Variant
Get the code

Section footer card button group

The button-group footer with card padding, for use inside a bordered card component.

Learn more
Variant
Get the code

Section footer card

The minimal footer with card padding, for the bottom of a card component.

Learn more

Anatomy & variants

How a section footer is composed, and which variant to reach for.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

ZoneContentsAlignment
Top borderA hairline separating the footer from the content aboveFull width
LeftOptional button group and a tertiary linkflex-start
RightSecondary and primary action buttonsflex-end (margin-left:auto)
Variants

Variants at a glance

VariantButton groupCard paddingLeft content
Section footerNoNoLink
Section footer button groupYesNoButton group + link
Section footer cardNoYesLink
Section footer card button groupYesYesButton group + link
Sub-components

Sub-components

ElementRoleBehaviour
Button groupradiogroupSingle-select segmented filter (one active at a time)
LinkTertiary / ghost linkContextual navigation, e.g. “Learn more”
Secondary buttonSecondary actionLower-emphasis commit action
Primary buttonPrimary actionThe main commit action
States

Interactive states

StateApplies toTreatment
DefaultAllBase colours
HoverAll interactiveBackground / text shift over 100ms
ActiveAll interactiveDeeper background, shadow collapses
Focus visibleAll interactive2px brand outline with 2px offset
SelectedButton group onlyFilled with the hover background
DisabledAll interactive50% opacity, not-allowed cursor
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The button group uses role="radiogroup" with each option as a role="radio" — a single-select pattern.
  • Arrow keys move between options; only the selected option is in the tab order (roving tabindex).
  • Every control shows a visible 2px brand focus ring with a 2px offset; rings are never suppressed.
  • The link carries an enlarged invisible hit target for comfortable touch use.
  • Layout follows a clear rule — filters and context on the left, commit actions on the right.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a section footer reads.

Top border / divider--hair
Button group surface--card
Selected option--raised
Link / secondary text--txt-2
Primary action--magenta
Card border--hair-2
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Keep one primary action and demote the rest to secondary.
  • Put filters and context on the left, commit actions on the right.
  • Use the card variants inside bordered cards, panels and drawers.
  • Use the section variants for full-width page-section footers.
  • Keep the button group single-select with a clear active option.
Don’t
  • Stack multiple primary buttons in one footer.
  • Mix the card padding with a full-width section footer.
  • Use the radio button group for multi-select — use checkboxes.
  • Crowd the bar so it wraps awkwardly on smaller screens.
  • Hide which button-group option is currently selected.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use the card variants when the footer sits inside a component with its own border (a data table, settings panel, modal or drawer); use the section variants for a full-width page section.

It is single-select — a radiogroup where one option is active at a time. For multi-select, swap to checkbox-style buttons.

The secondary and primary buttons are pushed to the far right with margin-left:auto, while filters and links stay on the left.

One. Keep a single primary action and express other choices as secondary buttons or links.

Yes — on small screens the bar wraps, the button group and actions go full width, and the actions stop being right-pinned.

Section headers

Powerful and customizable section header components for modern apps and websites — they sit at the top of a content section providing a title, optional description and action controls such as buttons, a search input, a button group, and navigation tabs. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Section header example

The full-featured default — a title and description with a search input, and a navigation tab bar below.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
⌘K
Get started

Installation

You can add this section header component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add section-headers
// copy the SectionHeader component into your project
import { SectionHeader } from "@/components/application/section-header";

Section header examples

Below are examples and variations of this section header component. Tabs and the button group are interactive — click to switch the active item.

Variant
Get the code

Section header buttons

A secondary and primary action with a more-options menu.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
Variant
Get the code

Section header search input

A standalone search input on the right.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Section header button group

A segmented button group filter with a more-options menu.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
Variant
Get the code

Section header buttons with tabs

Secondary and primary actions with a navigation tab bar below.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
Variant
Get the code

Section header search input with tabs

A search input with a navigation tab bar below.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.
⌘K
Variant
Get the code

Section header button groups with tabs

A segmented button group with a navigation tab bar below.

Team members
Manage your team members and their account permissions here.

Anatomy & variants

How a section header is composed, and which variant to reach for.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

SlotDescriptionRequired
TitleBold heading for the sectionYes
DescriptionSupporting text below the titleOptional
ActionsRight slot: buttons, search, button group or kebabOptional
TabsNavigation tabs below the title rowOptional
DividerHairline separating the header from content belowYes
Variants

Variants at a glance

VariantRight actionsTabs
Section header exampleSearch inputYes
Section header buttonsSecondary + Primary + kebabNo
Section header search inputSearch inputNo
Section header button groupButton group + kebabNo
Section header buttons with tabsSecondary + Primary + kebabYes
Section header search input with tabsSearch inputYes
Section header button groups with tabsButton group + kebabYes
Tabs

Tab bar & states

The optional tab bar uses an underline indicator for the active view and supports numeric badges.

StateAppearance
DefaultMuted text, no underline
HoverText darkens
ActiveStronger text with a brand-colour underline
With badgeA small numeric badge, e.g. Notifications 2
FocusVisible keyboard focus ring
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The tab bar uses role="tablist" with role="tab" items; the active tab is aria-selected="true".
  • Arrow keys move between tabs and the search field uses role="searchbox".
  • The kebab button carries an accessible aria-label and opens its menu on activation.
  • Every control shows a visible focus ring for keyboard users; rings are never suppressed.
  • The title is a single heading so screen-reader users land on the section name.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a section header reads.

Title text--txt
Description--txt-2
Divider / tab underline track--hair
Active tab underline--magenta
Primary action--magenta
Search / button-group surface--card
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use one at the top of each distinct content section.
  • Always include a title; add a description where it helps.
  • Use the buttons variant for one or two primary actions.
  • Use a search or button-group variant for filtering and toggles.
  • Use a “with tabs” variant when the section has sub-views.
Don’t
  • Crowd the action area with more than two buttons plus a kebab.
  • Nest a section header inside another section header.
  • Use tabs when there is only a single view.
  • Omit the divider line beneath the header.
  • Rely on colour alone to mark the active tab.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use buttons for one or two CTAs, a search input for filtering, a button group for time or view toggles, and a “with tabs” variant when the section has multiple sub-views.

No — tabs are optional. Use them only when a section splits into sub-views; otherwise a plain divider is enough.

Yes — all tab labels, the badge values and the active tab are configurable props.

On the right of the title row. Keep one primary action and express other choices as secondary buttons, a search field or a button group.

Yes — it uses a proper tablist with aria-selected on the active tab, a searchbox role on the input, a labelled kebab, and full keyboard navigation.

Sidebar navigations

Powerful and customizable sidebar navigation components for modern apps and websites — persistent vertical panels with branding, search, nav items, collapsible groups, badges, status indicators, external links, featured cards and a user-profile footer. Full, simple, dual-tier, slim, divider and subheading layouts. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Sidebar navigation example

The showcase variant — brand header, search, a collapsible group, a divider, a status badge, an external link and a user footer.

TTungo
⌘K
Home
Dashboard
Projects
Folders
Current work
Recently viewed
Shared with me
Reporting
Settings
SupportOnline
Open in browser
Caitlyn Kingcaitlyn@tungo.com
Get started

Installation

You can add this sidebar navigation component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add sidebar-navigation
// copy the SidebarNavigation component into your project
import { SidebarNavigation } from "@/components/application/sidebar-navigation";

Sidebar navigation examples

Below are examples and variations of this sidebar navigation component. Nav items, collapsible groups and card dismiss buttons are all interactive.

Streamlined nav items with expand chevrons and a count badge.

Featured cards

Modular inserts that sit inside the sidebar above the user footer. The two progress cards are free; the rest are PRO. Every card can be dismissed with the × button.

Inserts

Anatomy & decorators

How a sidebar is composed, and the decorators a nav item can carry.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

PartDescription
Brand headerApp logo and name at the top
Search barSearch field with a ⌘K shortcut (or a standalone icon)
Nav itemIcon + label, with optional right-side decorators
Collapsible groupA folder that expands to reveal child items
Divider / section labelSeparates logical groups of items
Featured cardAn optional insert above the footer
User footerAvatar, name, email and a menu chevron
Decorators

Nav item decorators

DecoratorUse
Expand chevronMarks a collapsible group
Count badgeA number, e.g. Tasks 10
Status badgeA dot + label, e.g. Online
External linkAn arrow for links that open elsewhere
Keyboard shortcutA shortcut hint, e.g. ⌘1
Right chevronNavigates into a sub-view, e.g. a team
States

Nav item states

StateTreatment
DefaultMuted icon and label on a transparent surface
HoverSubtle raised background; icon and text darken
Active / currentRaised background wash; stronger text (aria-current)
FocusVisible focus ring for keyboard users
Collapsed / expandedGroup chevron points down / up
DisabledReduced opacity, not interactive
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The panel is a navigation landmark; items use list semantics and links / buttons as appropriate.
  • The active item is marked aria-current="page"; collapsible groups toggle aria-expanded.
  • The search wrapper uses role="search" and the input is labelled.
  • Progress cards expose role="progressbar" with value attributes; dismiss buttons are labelled.
  • Status is conveyed with text alongside the colour dot, never colour alone; focus rings stay visible.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every layout and card adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a sidebar reads.

Sidebar surface--card
Item default text--txt-2
Item hover / active bg--raised
Divider--hair
Section label--txt-3
Brand / accent--magenta
Status online#19B36B
Card surface--raised
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Mark the current page with a clear active state.
  • Group related items with dividers or section labels.
  • Use the slim variant as a collapsed state with tooltips.
  • Keep one featured card at a time, above the footer.
  • Use badges and status dots with accompanying text.
Don’t
  • Stack several featured cards at once.
  • Bury primary navigation below long secondary lists.
  • Use icon-only items without tooltips or labels.
  • Rely on colour alone for status or the active item.
  • Nest collapsible groups more than one level deep.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use the full or dividers / subheadings layouts for feature-rich apps, simple for lighter products, dual-tier to separate utility nav, and slim as a collapsed state.

A folder item toggles open on click, rotating its chevron and revealing indented child items; the trigger exposes aria-expanded.

Modular inserts — progress, upgrade, support, referral and more — that sit above the footer and can be dismissed with the × button.

Yes — the slim variant is the icon-only collapsed state, typically paired with tooltips that reveal labels on hover.

Yes — it is a navigation landmark with aria-current on the active item, aria-expanded on groups, a labelled search, and full keyboard support.

Slideout menus

Powerful and customizable slideout menu (drawer) components for modern apps and websites — fixed overlay panels that slide in from the edge over a soft gradient backdrop, with a header, a scrollable body and a sticky footer. Dismissible by the close button, a backdrop click or Escape. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Slideout menu example

Click Open slideout to slide the panel in from the right. Dismiss it with the × button, the backdrop, or the Escape key.

Notifications
You have 5 unread updates.

12m
Lana Steiner
shared the “Q3 launch” board
1h
Demi Wilkinson
requested access to Designs
3h
Candice Wu
left feedback on your draft
5h
Get started

Installation

You can add this slideout menu component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add slideout-menu
// copy the SlideoutMenu component into your project
import { SlideoutMenu } from "@/components/application/slideout-menu";

Slideout menu examples

Below are examples and variations of this slideout menu component — each shown open over the dimmed backdrop. Toggles, checkboxes, chips, tabs and selects inside the panels are interactive.

Anatomy & structure

How a slideout is composed, and the rules it follows.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

PartDescription
BackdropA soft gradient overlay that dims the page behind the panel
PanelA fixed surface (~520px) anchored to an edge of the viewport
HeaderOptional leading icon, a title, a description and a close button
BodyA scrollable region holding the variant’s content
FooterA sticky bar with an optional link plus secondary and primary actions
Position & size

Position & sizing

PropertyOptions
PositionRight (default), left, top-right or bottom-left
Width~520px on desktop; full-width on mobile
Z-indexSits above page content on a dedicated overlay layer
ScrollThe body scrolls independently; header and footer stay fixed
States

States & dismissal

StateBehaviour
ClosedPanel off-screen, backdrop transparent and non-interactive
OpeningPanel slides in while the backdrop fades up (~360ms)
OpenPanel in place; focus is trapped inside the dialog
DismissClose button, a backdrop click, or the Escape key
ClosingPanel slides back out and the backdrop fades away
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The panel is a dialog with aria-modal="true" and a label from its title.
  • Focus moves into the panel on open, is trapped while open, and returns to the trigger on close.
  • Escape and a backdrop click both dismiss it; the close button is labelled.
  • The body scrolls independently while the page behind is locked from scrolling.
  • All controls inside keep visible focus rings and never rely on colour alone.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a slideout reads.

Panel surface--card
Backdropbrand gradient + dim
Header / footer border--hair
Primary action--magenta
Destructive action#F0433A
Toggle on / accent--magenta
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use for secondary tasks that keep the page context.
  • Keep one clear primary action in the footer.
  • Let the body scroll while the header and footer stay put.
  • Allow dismissal by backdrop click and the Escape key.
  • Slide from the right for actions, the left for navigation.
Don’t
  • Use a slideout for a primary, full-page workflow.
  • Stack multiple slideouts on top of each other.
  • Trap users — always provide a visible close affordance.
  • Overload the footer with more than one primary action.
  • Forget to return focus to the trigger on close.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Use a slideout for secondary tasks, detail views and forms that benefit from keeping page context; use a modal for short, focused confirmations.

Right is the default for actions and detail views; left suits navigation; top-right and bottom-left are available for notifications and toasts.

By the close button, a click on the backdrop, or the Escape key — focus then returns to the element that opened it.

Yes — the body scrolls independently while the header and footer stay fixed and the page behind is locked.

Yes — it is a labelled modal dialog with focus trapping, keyboard dismissal and visible focus rings throughout.

Tables

Free and open-source table components for modern apps and websites — accessible, sortable, selectable and paginated data tables with avatars, status badges, tag groups, progress bars, file-type icons and inline actions. Two densities, divider-line and alternating-fill styles, and full empty, error and offline states. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Table example

The default medium-density table — selectable rows with avatars, an Active status, role, email, team tags and inline actions, with numbered pagination.

Get started

Installation

You can add this table component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add table
// copy the Table components into your project
import { TableCard, Table } from "@/components/application/table";
Sizes

Sizes

Two densities are available via the size prop on TableCard.Root or Table — md (default, ~72px rows, 40px avatars) and sm (~56px rows, 32px avatars) for data-heavy views.

Table examples

Below are examples and variations of this table component. Rows are selectable, headers sortable, and every table scrolls horizontally on narrow screens. Use the checkboxes, sort headers, tabs and pagination to interact.

Size
Get the code

Table small size example

The compact sm density with smaller avatars and tighter rows, paired with the “Page X of Y” pagination and a per-page selector.

Anatomy & cell types

How a table is composed, and the content a cell can hold.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

PartDescription
Card headerTitle, count badge, secondary / primary CTAs and an overflow menu
ToolbarOptional view tabs, search and a filters button
Header rowSortable column headers with sort and info icons, plus a master checkbox
Body rowsSelectable, hoverable rows of cells with inline actions
Pagination footerNumbered pages, or “Page X of Y” with a per-page selector
Cells

Cell content types

TypeExample
Avatar + nameA round avatar with a name and @handle
Status badgeActive, Customer, Paid, Refunded, Cancelled, Churned
Tag groupTeam pills with a +N overflow
Progress barA track, fill and percentage label
Avatar stackOverlapping avatars with a +5 overflow
File iconA coloured file-type badge with name and size
Email / domainBrand-coloured link text
Inline actionsDelete and edit icons revealed on row hover
States

Row, header & checkbox states

StateTreatment
Row hoverRaised background fill; action icons fade in
Row selectedPersisted raised fill with a checked checkbox
Header sortableSort icon brightens on hover; toggles ascending / descending
Header infoAn info icon sits beside the label
Checkbox checkedBrand fill with a white check
Checkbox indeterminateBrand fill with a dash (some rows selected)
Pagination disabledPrevious on page 1 and Next on the last page are greyed
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a table reads.

Card surface--card
Row hover / selected / stripe--raised
Divider / border--hair
Brand / checkbox / progress--magenta
Active & paid badge#19B36B
Cancelled badge#F0433A
Email / domain link--magenta
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • Uses grid semantics — role="grid", row, columnheader and gridcell.
  • Sortable headers expose aria-sort; selected rows expose aria-selected.
  • Checkboxes are labelled; the master checkbox uses aria-checked="mixed" when partially selected.
  • Arrow keys move between cells, Space toggles selection, and Enter sorts a focused header.
  • Every interactive control keeps a visible focus ring; text meets AA contrast in both themes.
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use the small density for data-heavy views.
  • Let tables scroll horizontally on narrow screens.
  • Use status badges and progress bars to surface key data.
  • Provide clear empty, error and offline states.
  • Keep one primary action in the card header.
Don’t
  • Cram too many columns into a fixed width — allow scroll.
  • Hide the current page or selection state.
  • Mix divider lines and zebra stripes in one table.
  • Rely on colour alone for status — add a label.
  • Leave a blank body with no empty-state guidance.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

The md density uses ~72px rows and 40px avatars; the sm density tightens rows to ~56px with 32px avatars for data-heavy views. Set it with the size prop.

Both are visual styles for the same data — divider lines draw a rule under every row, while alternating fills use a zebra stripe. Don’t combine them.

Each row has a checkbox; the header carries a master checkbox that shows an indeterminate dash when only some rows are selected.

Yes — every table is wrapped in a horizontal scroll container, so on narrow screens it scrolls sideways instead of breaking the layout.

The component ships no-results, onboarding, error and offline states that replace the table body while keeping the card header.

Tabs

Powerful and customizable tab components for modern apps and websites — they let people switch between related sections of content in the same context. Six visual styles (button-brand, button-gray, button-border, button-minimal, underline and line) in horizontal and vertical orientations, with badges, sizes and full-width layouts. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS.

Example
Get the code

Tabs example

The default tabs — click any tab to switch the panel below. Notifications carries a count badge.

My details — manage your personal information, profile photo and the email address associated with your account. Select another tab to switch sections.
Get started

Installation

You can add this tabs component with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add tabs
// copy the Tabs component into your project
import { Tabs } from "@/components/application/tabs";

Tabs examples

Below are examples and variations of this tabs component. Every set is interactive — click a tab to select it.

Variant
Get the code

Button brand horizontal

A filled pill using the brand colour for the selected tab.

Variant
Get the code

Button gray horizontal

A filled neutral-gray pill for the selected tab.

Variant
Get the code

Button border horizontal

An outlined pill with a border on the selected tab.

Variant
Get the code

Button minimal horizontal

No background — selection shown by text colour and weight only.

Variant
Get the code

Underline

A classic bottom-border indicator under the selected tab.

Variant
Get the code

Button brand vertical

The brand pill style stacked vertically.

Variant
Get the code

Button gray vertical

The gray pill style stacked vertically.

Variant
Get the code

Button border vertical

The bordered style stacked vertically.

Variant
Get the code

Button minimal vertical

The minimal text style stacked vertically.

Variant
Get the code

Line

A vertical left-side bar indicating the selected tab.

Sizes, badges & full width

The same tabs in their medium size, and distributed evenly across the full container width.

Size
Get the code

Medium size

Larger padding for more breathing room (the default is small).

Layout
Get the code

Full width

Tabs stretch to fill and divide the container evenly.

Anatomy & variants

How a tab group is composed, and the six styles to choose from.

Anatomy

Anatomy elements

PartDescription
TabsRoot container managing selection, keyboard nav and ARIA roles
Tabs.ListThe row or column of tab triggers (type, size, orientation, fullWidth)
Tabs.ItemA single trigger — label, optional icon and optional badge
Tabs.PanelThe content region revealed when a tab is selected
Variants

The six styles

TypeSelected treatmentBest for
button-brandFilled brand pill, white textProminent, primary tab groups
button-grayFilled gray pill, dark textSecondary navigation inside panels
button-borderSurface fill with a border ringTabs over coloured backgrounds
button-minimalText colour and weight onlyMinimal, content-heavy pages
underlineColoured bottom borderTraditional content tabs
lineColoured left bar (vertical)Vertical sidebars and settings menus
States

States

StateTreatment
DefaultTransparent background, muted text
HoverSubtle tint on button styles; colour shift on underline/line
PressedA slightly deeper background, flat (no scale)
SelectedEach variant’s own indicator; label turns semibold
FocusA visible brand focus ring on keyboard navigation only
DisabledHeavily muted text, not interactive, skipped by arrow keys
Badges

Badge support

Any tab can carry a count or text badge. On a selected brand tab the badge flips to a white background with brand text; on underline and line tabs it uses a brand-tinted pill. Notifications shows a 2 in these examples.

Accessibility

Keyboard & accessibility

  • The list is a tablist; items are tabs with aria-selected, and panels are tabpanels.
  • Arrow keys move between tabs (←/→ horizontal, ↑/↓ vertical); Home and End jump to first / last.
  • Enter or Space selects the focused tab; the panel receives focus on switch for screen readers.
  • A visible brand focus ring appears on keyboard navigation only, never on mouse click.
  • Disabled tabs are skipped by arrow navigation and expose aria-disabled.
Responsive

Responsive behaviour

On wide screens the tab row is shown in full. On narrow screens the row wraps, and the recommended pattern is to swap to a native select on mobile so every option stays reachable with one tap.

Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every variant adapts to the active theme. These are the core tokens a tab group reads.

Selected (brand) fill--magenta
Selected (gray) fill--raised
Default text--txt-3
Selected text--txt
Underline / line indicator--magenta
Border / track--hair
Guidelines

Do’s and don’ts

Do
  • Use tabs for related content at the same level.
  • Keep labels short — one to three words.
  • Use badges for actionable counts like unread items.
  • Disable tabs (with a reason) instead of hiding them.
  • Use underline or line styles in content-heavy contexts.
Don’t
  • Use tabs as page-level (router) navigation.
  • Nest tabs inside tabs.
  • Exceed seven or eight tabs — use a dropdown instead.
  • Use tabs for sequential steps — use a stepper.
  • Hide critical content behind a non-default tab.
FAQs

Frequently asked questions

Control it with the selectedKey and onSelectionChange props on the root, or use defaultSelectedKey for an uncontrolled default.

Yes — pass a badge prop on a Tabs.Item with a number or short text; it restyles automatically when the tab is selected.

Two: small (the default, compact) and medium (more padding), set with the size prop on Tabs.List.

Set the fullWidth prop on Tabs.List and the tabs divide the container evenly.

Yes — they use a proper tablist with roving arrow-key navigation, Home/End support, aria-selected, focus management to the panel, and visible keyboard focus rings.

Log in pages

Powerful and customizable log in pages for modern apps and websites — the first interaction a person has with your product, where brand and experience are set. Seventeen production-ready layouts: simple centered, enclosed cards, and split screens with mockups, testimonials, imagery and carousels. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS. The featured example below is interactive — toggle the tabs, reveal the password and tick remember-me.

Example
Get the code

Log in page example

The featured layout — a simple centered form with a Sign up / Log in toggle, shown in an error state. Try it: switch tabs, click the eye to reveal the password, toggle the checkbox.

Log in to your account

Welcome back! Please enter your details.

Don’t have an account?

Log in is often the first interaction a person has with your product. This entry-point experience is an important moment in establishing your product’s brand and sets the tone for everything that follows.

Log in page examples

Below are production-ready log in page layouts. The simple and card layouts stay single-column; the split layouts collapse to just the form on small screens.

The clean base variant — identical structure to the featured example, in its default empty state.

Anatomy, layouts & states

How every log in page is composed, the three layout families, and the states each control moves through.

Anatomy

Page anatomy

ElementRequired
Logo / brand markYes
Title (H1) & subtitleYes
Sign up / Log in toggleOptional
Email inputYes
Password input with revealYes
Error badge (trailing)Conditional
Remember-me checkboxOptional
Forgot-password linkOptional
Primary “Sign in” buttonYes
Social login button(s)Optional
OR dividerOptional
SAML SSO linkOptional
Right panel (image / mockup / quote)Split only
Layouts

Layout categories

FamilyDescription
SimpleVertically centred column, no side panel, ~340–480px wide
CardForm enclosed in a card — separated (logo/title outside) or combined (all inside)
Split50/50 — form left, decorative panel right; the panel hides on mobile
Inputs

Input field states

StateTreatment
DefaultGray border, white surface, placeholder text
HoverBorder darkens
FocusBrand border with a soft brand ring
ErrorRed border, red ring, trailing error badge
PasswordMasked dots with an eye toggle; error badge and eye sit side by side
DisabledMuted surface, not editable
Buttons & links

Button, social & link states

ControlSelected / hover treatment
Primary “Sign in”Brand fill, darkens on hover, brand ring on focus
Secondary / GoogleWhite surface with border, gray tint on hover
Facebook / AppleProvider icon, same outlined button (Apple uses a dark fill option)
LinksBrand colour, underline on hover, brand ring on focus
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every layout adapts to the active theme. Tungo maps the source’s purple brand to its magenta.

Primary button / links--magenta
Page / input surface--card
Tab group / disabled bg--raised
Input border--hair
Heading text--txt
Error border / badge#F0433A
Responsive

Responsive behaviour

  • Simple and card layouts are always single-column — only horizontal padding changes.
  • Split layouts collapse to a single column below the large breakpoint, showing just the form.
  • The right image / mockup / quote panel is hidden on mobile (no horizontal page scroll).
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • Explicit labels on every input — placeholders are never used as the label.
  • Logical tab order: tabs → email → password → remember → forgot → sign in → social → footer.
  • The password toggle is a button with an aria-label that switches between Show and Hide password.
  • Errors use role=“alert” and aria-describedby; the toggle group exposes tablist semantics.
  • Visible brand focus rings on every interactive element; all text meets AA contrast.

Sign up pages

Sign up pages should be effortless and reassuring — clear, low-friction, and often paired with social proof to encourage the next step. Twenty-one production-ready layouts: simple, card, multi-step progress, split screens, and sidebar-progress flows. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS. The featured example below is interactive — toggle the tabs and reveal the password.

Example
Get the code

Sign up simple

The featured layout — a centered form with a Sign up / Log in toggle, name, email, password and live hints.

Create an account

Start your 30-day free trial.

Must be at least 8 characters.
Must contain one special character.

Already have an account?

Anatomy, layouts & states

How every sign-up page is composed, the layout families, and the states each control moves through.

Anatomy

Core form structure

ElementNotes
LogoCentred brand mark at the top
Heading“Create an account” / “Sign up”
Subheading“Start your 30-day free trial.”
Sign up / Log in toggleOptional segmented control
Name, Email, PasswordCore fields; password carries a reveal toggle
Password hints“Must be at least 8 characters” with check icons
Primary CTA“Get started” or “Continue”
Social button(s)Google, optionally Facebook & Apple
OR divider / SAMLOptional
Footer link“Already have an account? Log in”
Layouts

Layout patterns

PatternDescription
SimpleCentred form, no surrounding content
CardFields in a card — separated or combined
ProgressSequential steps with a dot, icon-label or line indicator
Split50/50 — form left, visual right (or reversed)
Sidebar progressDark step sidebar on the left, content on the right
Inputs & buttons

Field & button states

ControlTreatment
Input default / focusGray border → brand border with a soft ring on focus
Input errorRed border, red ring, trailing error badge
PasswordMasked dots, eye toggle, hint list below
PrimaryBrand fill, darkens on hover, brand ring on focus
Social / GoogleWhite surface with border, gray tint on hover
Progress

Progress indicators

  • Dots — a compact four-step dot row; completed dots and the active dot use the brand colour.
  • Icon & label — a horizontal stepper with an icon and caption per step; done steps show a check.
  • Line / segments — four bars that fill to brand as the person advances.
  • Sidebar — a vertical step list with icon, label and sublabel; completed, active and upcoming states.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every layout adapts to the active theme; the source’s purple brand maps to Tungo’s magenta.

Primary / links / active step--magenta
Surface / inputs--card
Tab group / hint bg--raised
Input border / track--hair
Heading text--txt
Error border / badge#F0433A
Responsive

Responsive behaviour

  • Simple, card and progress layouts are single-column — only padding changes.
  • Split and sidebar layouts collapse to the form alone below the large breakpoint.
  • The right visual panel and the step sidebar are hidden on mobile, so there is no horizontal page scroll.
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • Explicit labels on every field; the password toggle is a button with an aria-label.
  • Password hints are associated with the field and announced; errors use role=“alert”.
  • The Sign up / Log in toggle exposes tablist semantics; progress steps expose their state.
  • Visible brand focus rings on every interactive element; all text meets AA contrast.

Verification pages

Powerful and customizable email verification pages for modern apps and websites. Verification screens should be as simple as possible and free from distraction — no nav, no sidebar, just the next action. A three-step linear flow: check email → enter the code → success. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS. The code step below is interactive — type to auto-advance across the cells.

Example
Get the code

Verification page example

The featured screen — a clean “check your email” prompt with a single primary action.

Check your email

We sent a verification link to olivia@tungo.com

Email verification page designs should be as simple as possible and free from distractions.

Verification page examples

The three steps of the verification journey. The code step is fully interactive — typing auto-advances, Backspace steps back, and pasting a 4-digit code fills every cell.

Step 1
Get the code

Step 1: Check email

Prompts the person to open the verification link sent to their inbox.

Check your email

We sent a verification link to olivia@tungo.com

Step 2
Get the code

Step 2: Enter code manually

A four-digit one-time-code entry with auto-advance, backspace and paste support.

Check your email

We sent a verification link to olivia@tungo.com

Didn’t receive the email?

Step 3
Get the code

Step 3: Success

Confirms the address is verified and sends the person on their way.

Email verified

Your email has been successfully verified. Please click below to log in magically.

Didn’t receive the email?

Anatomy, the flow & states

How the screens are composed, the linear journey, and the states the code cells move through.

Anatomy

Shared anatomy

ElementNotes
Featured iconA gray “modern” icon (mail or check) with a faint grid backdrop on desktop
Heading“Check your email” or “Email verified”
BodyOne or two lines of muted guidance, with the email emphasised
Code cellsFour single-digit inputs (step 2 only)
Primary buttonFull-width — Enter code manually / Verify email / Continue
Resend link“Didn’t receive the email? Click to resend” (steps 2 & 3)
Back link“Back to log in” with a leading arrow
Flow

The three-step journey

  • Step 1 — Check email. A link was sent; the person can switch to manual code entry.
  • Step 2 — Enter code. Four cells accept the one-time code, with resend available.
  • Step 3 — Success. The email is verified and the person continues into the product.
Code cells

OTP cell states

StateTreatment
Default / emptyGray border, white surface, dimmed 0 placeholder
FocusBrand border with a soft brand ring; caret shown
FilledBrand-tinted border, dark digit
Filled + focusBrand border, ring and dark digit together
ErrorRed border and ring
DisabledMuted surface, not editable
Behaviour

Code-entry behaviour

  • Typing a digit auto-advances focus to the next cell.
  • Backspace clears and moves focus back to the previous cell.
  • Pasting a full four-digit code fills every cell at once.
  • Only numeric input is accepted; mobile shows a numeric keypad.
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Both screens adapt to the active theme; the source’s purple brand maps to Tungo’s magenta.

Primary button / cell focus / links--magenta
Page / icon / cell surface--card
Heading text--txt
Body text--txt-2
Cell border / grid pattern--hair
Responsive

Responsive behaviour

  • A single centred column at every size; only padding and heading size change.
  • The decorative grid pattern behind the icon is hidden on mobile to reduce noise.
  • The code cells shrink slightly on small screens but stay comfortably tappable.
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • The code cells form a labelled group; each cell has its own aria-label.
  • Focus auto-advances on entry and returns on Backspace for fast, predictable entry.
  • Numeric input mode triggers the numeric keypad on mobile.
  • Visible brand focus rings on every interactive element; all text meets AA contrast.

Forgot password pages

Powerful and customizable password-reset pages for modern apps and websites. Reset flows should be as simple as possible and free from distraction. A four-step linear journey — request a link, check email, set a new password, success — plus a sidebar layout. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS. The set-password step below is interactive: click the eye to reveal each field.

Example
Get the code

Forgot password page example

The featured screen — ask for the email and send reset instructions.

Forgot password?

No worries, we’ll send you reset instructions.

Password reset page designs should be as simple as possible and free from distractions.

Forgot password page examples

The four steps of the reset journey, plus a sidebar variant. The set-password step is interactive — reveal either field with the eye toggle.

Step 1
Get the code

Step 1: Forgot password

Request a reset link with the account email.

Forgot password?

No worries, we’ll send you reset instructions.

Step 2
Get the code

Step 2: Check email

Confirm the link was sent; open the inbox or resend.

Check your email

We sent a password reset link to olivia@tungo.com

Didn’t receive the email?

Step 3
Get the code

Step 3: Set new password

Choose a new password that differs from previous ones.

Set new password

Your new password must be different to previously used passwords.

Must be at least 8 characters.
Must contain one special character.
Step 4
Get the code

Step 4: Success

Confirm the reset and send the person back in.

Password reset

Your password has been successfully reset. Click below to log in magically.

Sidebar
Get the code

Step sidebar version

An alternative layout for step one — an onboarding step sidebar on the left, the form on the right.

Untitled UI
Your detailsPlease provide your name and email
Company detailsA few details about your company
Choose a passwordChoose a secure password
Add your socialsShare posts to your social accounts
© Tungo 2077help@tungo.com

Forgot password?

No worries, we’ll send you reset instructions.

Anatomy, the flow & states

How the screens are composed, the linear journey, and the states each control moves through.

Anatomy

Shared anatomy

ElementNotes
Featured iconA gray “modern” icon — key, mail, lock or check — with a faint grid backdrop on desktop
Heading & body“Forgot password?” / “Set new password” with one line of guidance
Email fieldStep 1 only — label plus a single input
Password + confirmStep 3 — two masked inputs with eye toggles and a strength checklist
Primary buttonFull-width — Reset password / Open email app / Continue
Resend link“Didn’t receive the email? Click to resend” (step 2)
Back link“Back to log in” with a leading arrow
Sidebar (variant)A 384px onboarding step list with a footer, beside the form
Flow

The four-step journey

  • Step 1 — Forgot password. Enter the account email to request reset instructions.
  • Step 2 — Check email. A reset link was sent; open the inbox or resend.
  • Step 3 — Set new password. Choose and confirm a new, sufficiently strong password.
  • Step 4 — Success. The password is reset and the person continues back in.
Inputs

Field states

StateTreatment
Default / focusGray border → brand border with a soft ring on focus
ErrorRed border, red ring and a trailing error badge
PasswordMasked dots with an eye toggle; error badge and eye sit side by side
ChecklistEach rule shows a filled brand check once satisfied
Theming

Dark mode & tokens

Every screen adapts to the active theme; the source’s purple brand maps to Tungo’s magenta.

Primary button / focus / links--magenta
Page / icon / input surface--card
Sidebar fillbrand gradient
Heading text--txt
Border / grid pattern--hair
Error border / badge#F0433A
Responsive

Responsive behaviour

  • A single centred column at every size; only padding and heading size change.
  • The grid pattern behind the icon is hidden on mobile to reduce noise.
  • The sidebar variant collapses to just the form below the large breakpoint — no horizontal page scroll.
Accessibility

Accessibility

  • Explicit labels on every field; each password toggle is a button with an aria-label.
  • The strength checklist is associated with the field and announced as rules are met.
  • Errors use role=“alert” and link to their input via aria-describedby.
  • Visible brand focus rings on every interactive element; all text meets AA contrast.

404 sections

Powerful and customizable 404 (page-not-found) sections for modern apps and websites. A dead end should never feel like one — clear messaging, no jargon, and always at least one path home. Fourteen production-ready layouts: simple, centered, split-image, illustration and screen-mockup variants. Built with React Aria, styled with Tailwind CSS. The source’s purple brand maps to Tungo’s magenta throughout.

Example
Get the code

404 section example

The flagship pattern — a purple “404 error” label, an extra-bold heading, supporting copy and a Go back / Take me home action pair, left-aligned.

404 error

We can’t find that page

Sorry, the page you are looking for doesn’t exist or has been moved.

Get started

Installation

You can add any 404 section with the CLI, or copy it in manually.

npx tungo-ui@latest add not-found-simple-01
import { NotFound } from "@/components/application/not-found";

404 section examples

Below are the fourteen production-ready layouts. Simple and centered variants stay single-column; split, illustration and mockup variants hide their right panel on small screens, so there is never any horizontal page scroll.

Simple
Get the code

Simple 01

Left-aligned with a purple text label and a Go back / Take me home pair.
